results.title: Lack of access control in AgentNftV2::addValidator() enables unauthorized validator injection and causes reward accounting inconsistencies
results.reasoning: Yes. The report explicitly flags the same root issue in the same code path. Item 37 identifies that AgentNftV2.addValidator() is permissionless (lack of access control), enabling unauthorized validator registration. Item 51 complements this by noting that AgentNftV2.mint() calls _addValidator without a uniqueness check, allowing duplicate entries when a validator was pre-added. This matches the described attack path (pre-adding a validator before mint leads to duplicates) even though the report emphasizes score inflation/DoS rather than the rewardPool mis-accounting effect. The same specific fix (adding access control to addValidator and/or enforcing uniqueness) would mitigate the described vulnerability, so it is detected.

results.title: Public ServiceNft::updateImpact call leads to cascading issue
results.reasoning: Yes. Audit item 13 (“ServiceNft Impact Score Griefing via Public updateImpact”) describes the same root cause: the updateImpact function in ServiceNft is public and lacks access control, allowing anyone to mutate impact scores. Both descriptions reference the same function/code path and the same effect of arbitrary changes to impacts that feed into rewards/minting. The proposed/implicit fix (restricting access to updateImpact, e.g., making it internal/only-governance) would resolve both. While the audit highlights zeroing impacts and the description emphasizes changes via datasetImpactWeight, both attacks exploit the same missing access control on the same function, so fixing one fixes the other.

results.title: Public ContributionNft::mint leads to cascading issues / loss of funds
results.reasoning: Yes. Audit item 25 describes the same issue in ContributionNft.mint: insufficient access control/validation allowing the proposal’s proposer to mint a Contribution NFT and set arbitrary fields (coreId, isModel, datasetId, parent linkage) without requiring proposal acceptance. It explains the downstream corruption of ServiceNft state and reward/impact calculations, matching the cascading effects cited (incorrect cores/models/datasets affecting ServiceNft and reward distribution). Both target the same function and mechanism (unguarded mint with unvalidated inputs), and addressing this (e.g., gating or adding state checks) would mitigate the described attack.

results.title: ValidatorRegistry::validatorScore/getPastValidatorScore allows validator to earn full rewards without actually engaging with the protocol
results.reasoning: Yes. Item 37 explicitly identifies that addValidator is permissionless and, via ValidatorRegistry::_initValidatorScore, initializes a new validator’s base score to the current max (totalProposals). It notes this inflates validatorScore and corrupts reward-related scoring. This is the same root cause and code path as the described issue (base score initialized to totalProposals; validatorScore/getPastValidatorScore add this base), and fixing the base-score initialization to 0 (or otherwise basing it on engagement) would resolve both. While the audit also mentions unbounded validator growth/DoS, it clearly captures the same scoring flaw enabling unearned rewards.
